name: Resistance training improved strength and reversed a mitochondrial-enriched aging signature
summary: Resistance training adds a second human functional exercise signal, though the mitochondrial evidence is transcriptomic and not a direct dynamics endpoint.
statement: In healthy older adults, six months of resistance training significantly improved strength and shifted many age-associated skeletal-muscle transcriptome changes, including a mitochondrial-function-enriched aging signature, closer to younger-adult levels.
population_or_model: Healthy older adults compared with younger adults; paired pre/post intervention subset of 14 older adults
quantitative_note: Older adults were 59% weaker than younger adults before training and 38% weaker after training; 179 age-associated genes were significantly associated with the exercise response.
caveats: The paired training subset was small and not a large randomized aging trial.
The mitochondrial signal is transcriptomic and pathway-enriched, not a direct clinical mitochondrial-function endpoint.
Strength benefit is modality-specific and should not be generalized to all biogenesis or dynamics interventions.
